source.title: Beta-band oscillations -- signalling the status quo?

confidence_rationale: Influential review directly articulating the 'status quo' function of beta oscillations across motor and cognitive circuits — specifically distinguishing beta as a signal that maintains current state against change, not merely an idle rhythm. High source quality and conceptual precision; the framing directly maps to MECH-090's committed-sequence gating. Main transfer risk: the review argues beta reflects a top-down maintenance signal but does not directly distinguish internal-update blocking from output-propagation blocking, which is the REE-specific claim.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Beta oscillations (~13-30 Hz) actively signal 'the status quo' — they maintain the current sensorimotor or cognitive state against disruptive change. Beta is not merely an idle rhythm but a top-down active maintenance signal: elevated during committed states, suppressed when the system must update or initiate change. Beta desynchronization precedes and accompanies voluntary movement; resynchronization marks the end of the action and restoration of the maintained state.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-090: Elevated beta during a committed action sequence is an active gate on E3 policy-output propagation — it keeps the current committed plan in force and prevents premature re-planning. The sequence-completion beta resynchronization that Engel & Fries describe as 'restoring the maintained state' maps to the post-sequence beta-drop that allows E3's updated state to propagate to action selection. Beta here is not idling but committed-state maintenance, directly supporting MECH-090's distinction from idle/default rhythm.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The review focuses primarily on sensorimotor beta and does not explicitly test the internal-model-update vs output-propagation distinction central to MECH-090. The argument that beta is a top-down maintenance signal (not bottom-up idling) supports the committed-sequence framing, but the precise locus of gating (E3 output vs downstream action selection) requires the 2019 Helfrich/Knight review for circuit-level corroboration.
mapping.source_context: Review of human EEG, MEG, and primate LFP studies; motor cortex, sensorimotor, and prefrontal regions; voluntary movement, cognitive control, and sensory change-detection paradigms.

confidence_components.notes: Very high source quality — canonical framing paper cited broadly for the status-quo interpretation; mapping to committed-sequence gating is conceptually tight; transfer risk is moderate since the output-propagation vs internal-update distinction is not explicitly tested.
